summary: This post collects Hillel Wayne's impressions of talks he attended at the final Strangeloop 2023 conference. He covers topics ranging from career advice and play in engineering to LLMs, stack-based languages, comedy with generative models, permacomputing sustainability, formal methods accessibility, and teaching difficult concepts. The post concludes with a personal reflection on how Strangeloop shaped his career and the unique community it fostered. Wayne notes that while a successor conference might emerge, it cannot replicate what made Strangeloop special—it was fundamentally Alex Miller's vision.
key_points: Stack-based languages have elegant properties: programs naturally compose since they're functions of type Stack -> Stack, and stack operations are associative regardless of how you partition the program
The tension in permacomputing lies between sustainability ideals and practical achievability—there's a spectrum between extreme minimalism and wasteful consumption that remains undefined
Making formal methods accessible is crucial; new languages like Fault aim to lower the barrier to entry for reasoning about systems
The best teaching insight from the conference: 'behind every best practice is a gruesome story'
Strangeloop's unique value was its eclectic mix of academia, industry, art, and activism—something difficult to replicate because it was one person's specific vision
money_quotes: Between the 'hurr dur you must be in it for the passion' people and the 'hurr durr smash capitalism' people, it's nice to hear some acknowledgement that money makes your life nice.

Stack ops are associative: if you chop a stack program into subprograms and pipe them into each other, it doesn't matter where you make the cuts, you still get the same final stack. That's really, really cool.
I'm painfully aware of just how hard grokking FM can be, and anything that makes it more accessible is good.
When she said that the hardest part is output formatting I felt it in my soul.
Behind every best practice is a gruesome story.
I consider myself a very good teacher. This talk made me a better one.
There are lots of people can run a good conference, but there's only one person who can run Alex's conference.
Whatever comes next will be fundamentally different. Still good, I'm sure, but different.
key_insight: Great conferences, like great software, reflect a singular vision that cannot be replicated—only honored through something fundamentally different.
